column1: Non-depolarising NMBAs act as {{c1::<u>competitive antagonists</u>}} at {{c2::<u>nicotinic ACh receptors</u>}} on the motor endplate → prevent ACh binding → no endplate depolarisation → no muscle contraction.
At the NMJ, arrival of an action potential at the axon terminal causes {{c1::<u>Ca²⁺ influx</u>}} → vesicle fusion → ACh release into the synaptic cleft.
ACh binds muscle nicotinic receptors → opens {{c1::Na⁺ channels}} → muscle {{c2::depolarisation}} → contraction. NMBAs block this final step.
ACh in the synaptic cleft is rapidly broken down by {{c1::acetylcholinesterase}}. Anticholinesterases exploit this to reverse non-depolarising block.
NMBAs have no intrinsic {{c1::<u>sedative, amnesic, or analgesic</u>}} effects — {{c2::<u>consciousness</u>}} and {{c3::<u>pain perception</u>}} are unaffected by paralysis.
In severe ARDS, NMBAs reduce {{c1::chest wall tone}} → ↓ {{c2::intrathoracic pressure}} → improved ventilation. One of the key ICU indications.
Patients receiving NMBAs require regular {{c1::<u>eye care</u>}} due to absent {{c2::<u>blink reflex</u>}}, risking {{c3::<u>corneal injury</u>}}.
Before commencing NMBAs in ICU, exhaust {{c1::sedation, analgesia, and ventilator optimisation}} first. NMBAs do not treat the underlying cause of distress.
